decisions.title: V1 MVP target date
decisions.detail: The primary goal for Version 1 of the Phoenix Project is to deliver a working Minimum Viable Product (MVP) by September 1st.

evidence.quote: The main goal for V1 is a working MVP by September 1st.

decisions.title: V1 deadline is a hard constraint
decisions.detail: The September 1st deadline for V1 MVP is a hard constraint due to a planned marketing campaign launch on September 5th.

evidence.quote: We have a hard constraint on the deadline because of the marketing campaign launch on Sept 5th.

decisions.title: Marketing ads booked
decisions.detail: Marketing advertisements related to the project have already been booked, reinforcing the hard deadline.

evidence.quote: Yes, we've already booked the ads.

decisions.title: V1 core features
decisions.detail: Version 1 of the project must include user authentication and a functional project dashboard.

evidence.quote: V1 must have user authentication and a working project dashboard.

decisions.title: Performance requirement for V1
decisions.detail: The system, particularly V1, must be 'super fast'. This is a non-functional performance requirement reflecting a business need.

evidence.quote: Let's make sure it's super fast.

decisions.title: Frontend technology choice
decisions.detail: React has been chosen for the frontend development stack.

evidence.quote: we are using React for the frontend

decisions.title: Backend technology choice
decisions.detail: Python with FastAPI has been chosen for the backend service development stack.

evidence.quote: Python (FastAPI) for the backend service.

decisions.title: Reason for Python backend
decisions.detail: Python was chosen for the backend due to existing libraries and team familiarity.

evidence.quote: I think it makes the most sense for our team since we already have libraries built in Python.

decisions.title: Database technology choice
decisions.detail: PostgreSQL will be used as the database, adhering to a standard relational model.

evidence.quote: We'll use PostgreSQL. Standard relational model is fine.

decisions.title: Session management technology
decisions.detail: A question was raised regarding whether to use Redis or standard database sessions for managing user sessions.

evidence.quote: But wait, what about session management? Should we use Redis or just standard database sessions?

decisions.title: Redis deferred to V2
decisions.detail: The use of Redis for session management will be deferred to V2, unless it proves to be a performance blocker for V1.

evidence.quote: Let's defer Redis to V2 unless it's a performance blocker.

decisions.title: Session management for V1
decisions.detail: For V1, standard PostgreSQL database sessions will be used for session management to keep it simple and save budget.

evidence.quote: Let's stick to standard postgres sessions for now to save budget.

decisions.title: Confirm PostgreSQL for sessions
decisions.detail: PostgreSQL is confirmed as the mechanism for managing user sessions.

evidence.quote: Okay, postgres for sessions.

decisions.title: User authentication implementation method
decisions.detail: Question about the specific implementation method for user authentication.

evidence.quote: How are we doing user auth?

decisions.title: External authentication provider preferred
decisions.detail: The preference is to use an external authentication provider like Auth0 or Firebase Auth, rather than building authentication in-house.

evidence.quote: We should probably use Auth0 or Firebase Auth. We don't want to build it ourselves, right?

decisions.title: Firebase Authentication chosen for V1
decisions.detail: Firebase Authentication has been chosen for user authentication in V1.

evidence.quote: for V1, let's just integrate Firebase Authentication.

decisions.title: Reasons for choosing Firebase Auth
decisions.detail: Firebase Authentication was selected due to its quick integration, ease of handling social logins, and alignment with pricing requirements.

evidence.quote: It's quick, handles social logins easily, and matches our pricing requirements.

decisions.title: Firebase Auth login methods
decisions.detail: Firebase Authentication will be configured to support Google sign-in and email-based logins.

evidence.quote: We will configure it to allow Google sign-in and email logins.

decisions.title: User registration acceptance criteria and data fields
decisions.detail: A question regarding the acceptance criteria for user registration and the specific data fields to be collected during the process.

evidence.quote: Do we have acceptance criteria for user registration? What fields do we collect?

decisions.title: Initial user registration fields
decisions.detail: Initially, user registration will collect email, full name, and role. Address and telephone number are not required at this stage.

evidence.quote: Just email, full name, and role. We don't need address or telephone number for now.

decisions.title: Company name required for B2B registration
decisions.detail: As the project is B2B, company name is a required field for user registration.

evidence.quote: Wait, we need to collect company name too! We are B2B.

decisions.title: Confirmation of company name requirement
decisions.detail: It is confirmed that company name is a required field during user signup.

evidence.quote: Company name is required during signup.

decisions.title: Final user registration fields
decisions.detail: The confirmed fields for user registration are email, full name, role, and company name.

evidence.quote: Email, full name, role, and company name.

decisions.title: Proposed deployment platforms
decisions.detail: John proposed Cloud Run for the FastAPI backend and Vercel for the React frontend deployment.

evidence.quote: I was thinking Cloud Run for the FastAPI app and Vercel for the React app.

decisions.title: Vercel approved, Cloud Run security questioned
decisions.detail: Vercel is acceptable for frontend deployment. A question was raised regarding the security of Cloud Run for backend deployment.

evidence.quote: Vercel is fine, but for the backend, is Cloud Run secure?

decisions.title: Database network security solution
decisions.detail: The PostgreSQL database instance will be placed in a private VPC and connected to Cloud Run via Serverless VPC Access to prevent exposure to the public internet.

evidence.quote: we will place the database in a private VPC and connect Cloud Run via Serverless VPC Access. That way the postgres instance is not exposed to the public internet.
evidence.approximate_location: John's statement about Cloud Run security

decisions.title: Database non-public exposure is a strict security constraint
decisions.detail: Ensuring the PostgreSQL instance is not exposed to the public internet is a strict technical constraint for security.

evidence.quote: This is a strict technical constraint for security.
evidence.approximate_location: John's statement about Cloud Run security

decisions.title: Thorough testing of authentication flow
decisions.detail: The authentication flow needs to be tested thoroughly.

evidence.quote: We need to test the auth flow thoroughly.

decisions.title: Session duration for logged-in users
decisions.detail: A question about the duration of a user session after login.

evidence.quote: If a user logs in, how long does the session last?

decisions.title: Token expiration set to 24 hours
decisions.detail: The token expiration for user sessions will be set to 24 hours, with the possibility of adjustment later.

evidence.quote: Let's set token expiration to 24 hours. We can adjust it later.

decisions.title: Dashboard metrics to display
decisions.detail: A question about the specific metrics that will be displayed on the project dashboard.

evidence.quote: What about the dashboard? What metrics are we displaying?

decisions.title: Dashboard content requirements
decisions.detail: The project dashboard must display active user metrics, system status, and project lists.

evidence.quote: We need active user metrics, system status, and project lists.

decisions.title: Detail dashboard features in next sync
decisions.detail: The detailed features of the dashboard will be discussed and defined in the next synchronisation meeting.

evidence.quote: Let's detail the dashboard features in the next sync.

decisions.title: Firebase provisioning ownership
decisions.detail: An open question regarding who is responsible for Firebase provisioning and the delivery of config keys.

evidence.quote: One open question: who is handling the Firebase provisioning?

decisions.title: Firebase config keys deadline
decisions.detail: The Firebase configuration keys are needed by next week.

evidence.quote: I need the config keys by next week.

decisions.title: Firebase provisioning assigned to Mike
decisions.detail: Mike will be assigned the task of Firebase provisioning.

evidence.quote: I'll assign that to Mike.

decisions.title: Firebase config keys delivery by July 8th
decisions.detail: Mike is responsible for delivering the Firebase configuration keys by July 8th.

evidence.quote: He should have it ready by July 8th.

decisions.title: Sync with marketing on dashboard design
decisions.detail: A sync meeting with the marketing team to discuss the dashboard design will occur next Wednesday.

evidence.quote: Let's make sure we sync with the marketing team on the dashboard design next Wednesday.

implementation_scope: Implement user authentication using Firebase Authentication, supporting Google sign-in and email-based logins.
Develop user registration functionality to collect email, full name, role, and company name.
Build a functional project dashboard component.
Develop backend APIs using Python with FastAPI.
Integrate PostgreSQL for data persistence and user session management.
Configure user session token expiration to 24 hours.
Implement dashboard views to display active user metrics, system status, and project lists.

constraints.text: Deliver V1 Minimum Viable Product (MVP) by September 1st (hard deadline due to marketing campaign).
System, particularly V1, must be 'super fast' (non-functional performance requirement).
PostgreSQL database instance must be placed in a private VPC and not exposed to the public internet (strict security constraint).
Firebase configuration keys are needed by July 8th (dependency on Mike for provisioning).
Detailed dashboard features will be defined in a future synchronization meeting with marketing.

architectural_decisions.text: Backend service development stack: Python with FastAPI.
Database: PostgreSQL, adhering to a standard relational model.
User session management for V1: Standard PostgreSQL database sessions (defer Redis to V2 unless performance bottleneck).
Backend deployment platform: Cloud Run, connected to PostgreSQL via Serverless VPC Access for security.

acceptance_criteria: Given a user navigates to the registration page, When they provide a unique email, a valid full name, a role, and a company name, Then their account is successfully created, and they are automatically logged in.

Given a a new user navigates to the registration page, When they attempt to register without providing a 'Company Name', Then the system prevents registration and displays a clear error message indicating that 'Company Name' is required.

Given a new user navigates to the registration page, When they attempt to register with an email address that is already in use, Then the system prevents registration and displays a clear error message indicating the email is taken.
Given a user navigates to the login page, When they select 'Sign in with Google' and successfully authenticate via their Google account, Then the user is successfully logged into the application.
Given a user navigates to the login page, When they enter valid email and password credentials, Then the user is successfully logged into the application.

Given a user is successfully logged in, When 24 hours have elapsed since their last login or activity, Then their session is automatically invalidated, and subsequent actions require re-authentication.
Given a user is logged in and navigates to the project dashboard, Then the dashboard page loads successfully and prominently displays a section for 'Active User Metrics'.
Given a user is logged in and navigates to the project dashboard, Then the dashboard page loads successfully and prominently displays a section for 'System Status'.
Given a user is logged in and navigates to the project dashboard, Then the dashboard page loads successfully and prominently displays a section for 'Project Lists'.
test_scenarios: Verify successful new user registration using unique email, full name, role, and company name, followed by automatic login.
Verify user login using Google authentication, including redirection to Google and successful return to the application.
Verify user login using email and password for a pre-registered account.
Verify that attempting to register with an email address already associated with an account results in an appropriate error message and prevents registration.
Verify that attempting to register with a blank 'Company Name' field results in an error message and prevents registration.
Verify that attempting to register with any other required field (Email, Full Name, Role) left blank results in an error message and prevents registration.
Verify that attempting to log in with an incorrect email or password results in an error message and prevents login.
Verify that after a user logs in, their session persists for 24 hours, and then expires, requiring re-login.
Verify that the project dashboard loads correctly and displays distinct sections for 'Active User Metrics', 'System Status', and 'Project Lists' without content errors.
edge_cases: Test user registration and login with email addresses, full names, roles, and company names containing maximum allowed characters.
Test user registration and login with email addresses, full names, roles, and company names containing special characters (e.g., accented letters, symbols permitted by Firebase/PostgreSQL).
Test concurrent logins for the same user account from multiple browsers or devices to observe session management behavior.
Test user registration with malformed email addresses (e.g., missing '@', missing domain, multiple '@' symbols).
Test login attempts with a valid email but an empty or excessively long password.
Observe dashboard load times and responsiveness when displaying a very large number of projects in the 'Project Lists' section.
unresolved_behaviors: PM/QA: What are the specific quantifiable performance targets for the 'super fast' requirement, particularly for user authentication flows (login, registration) and initial dashboard loading?
PM/QA: What are the specific data points, definitions, and expected values for 'Active User Metrics', 'System Status', and 'Project Lists' to be displayed on the project dashboard (as per D033)?
PM/QA: Are there any specific validation rules or a predefined list for the 'Role' field during user registration?
PM/QA: What are the password complexity requirements for email-based user registration (e.g., minimum length, requirement for numbers/symbols, uppercase/lowercase)?
PM/QA: What is the expected behavior for session management when a user explicitly logs out? Does it immediately invalidate the session token, or does the 24-hour timer still apply to the token?

decision_history: The primary goal for Version 1 (V1) of the Phoenix Project is to deliver a working Minimum Viable Product (MVP) by September 1st, which is a hard constraint due to a planned marketing campaign launch on September 5th.
Marketing advertisements related to the project have already been booked, reinforcing the hard V1 deadline.
V1 must include user authentication and a functional project dashboard.
A non-functional performance requirement for V1 is that the system must be 'super fast'.
React has been chosen for the frontend development stack.
Python with FastAPI has been chosen for the backend service due to existing libraries and team familiarity.
PostgreSQL will be used as the database, adhering to a standard relational model.

The use of Redis for session management will be deferred to V2, unless it proves to be a performance blocker for V1. For V1, standard PostgreSQL database sessions will be used to keep it simple and save budget.
The preference is to use an external authentication provider like Auth0 or Firebase Auth rather than building in-house.
Firebase Authentication has been chosen for user authentication in V1 due to its quick integration, ease of handling social logins, and alignment with pricing requirements.
Firebase Authentication will be configured to support Google sign-in and email-based logins.
User registration fields are confirmed as email, full name, role, and company name (company name is required as it's a B2B project).

Vercel is approved for frontend deployment. For backend deployment, the PostgreSQL database instance will be placed in a private VPC and connected to Cloud Run via Serverless VPC Access to prevent public internet exposure, fulfilling a strict technical security constraint.
The token expiration for user sessions will be set to 24 hours, with the possibility of adjustment later.
The project dashboard must display active user metrics, system status, and project lists. Detailed dashboard features will be discussed and defined in the next synchronization meeting.
Mike has been assigned the task of Firebase provisioning and is responsible for delivering the Firebase configuration keys by July 8th.
dependencies: Project MVP delivery by September 1st is dependent on the Marketing team's planned campaign launch on September 5th, with ads already booked.
Mike needs to provision Firebase and deliver configuration keys by July 8th for authentication development to proceed.
A sync meeting with the marketing team on 'next Wednesday' is required to discuss dashboard design, which will inform the detailed dashboard features.
risks: Missing the September 1st MVP deadline (High Impact, Medium Likelihood if scope isn't tightly managed): The deadline is a hard constraint due to a marketing campaign launching September 5th, with ads already booked. Any delay could severely impact marketing efforts.

Performance bottleneck with PostgreSQL sessions (High Impact, Low Likelihood): The 'super fast' performance requirement for V1 combined with deferring Redis for sessions to V2 introduces a risk that standard PostgreSQL sessions might not meet performance expectations, potentially requiring rework or delaying V1 if it becomes a blocker.

Delay in Firebase configuration keys (High Impact, Medium Likelihood): The Firebase configuration keys are needed by July 8th. Any delay in Mike delivering these could block development of the user authentication feature.
alignment_gaps: The non-functional requirement for the system to be 'super fast' is subjective and needs clearer definition/metrics to ensure shared understanding and avoid scope creep or quality disputes later.

Detailed features for the project dashboard are still to be defined and will be discussed in the next sync meeting. While high-level metrics are known, the specifics could lead to differing expectations if not fully aligned before development.
open_questions: Do we have acceptance criteria for user registration beyond the collected fields (email, full name, role, company name)? (Owned by PM/QA)

What are the specific, detailed metrics and functionalities to be displayed on the project dashboard, beyond active user metrics, system status, and project lists? (Owned by PM/Product, to be addressed in next sync)
milestones: Deliver a working Minimum Viable Product (MVP) by 2024-09-01.
Firebase configuration keys to be delivered by Mike by 2024-07-08.
Sync meeting with the marketing team on dashboard design to occur next Wednesday (date not specified, but a scheduled checkpoint).
Next synchronization meeting to define detailed dashboard features (date not specified, but a scheduled checkpoint).

system_requirements: The system SHALL provide user authentication. (D004)
The system SHALL provide a functional project dashboard. (D004)
The system MUST achieve 'super fast' performance. (D005)
Firebase Authentication SHALL be configured to support Google sign-in. (D018)
Firebase Authentication SHALL be configured to support email-based logins. (D018)
The user registration process SHALL collect the user's email address. (D020, D023)
The user registration process SHALL collect the user's full name. (D020, D023)
The user registration process SHALL collect the user's role. (D020, D023)
The user registration process SHALL collect the user's company name. (D021, D022, D023)
The authentication flow MUST be thoroughly tested. (D028)
User session tokens SHALL have an expiration duration of 24 hours. (D030)
The project dashboard SHALL display active user metrics. (D032)
The project dashboard SHALL display system status. (D032)
The project dashboard SHALL display project lists. (D032)
data_flows: Frontend (React) to Firebase Authentication: User authentication requests (e.g., Google sign-in, email/password credentials). (D016, D018)
Firebase Authentication to Frontend (React): Authentication tokens and user profile data. (D016, D018)
Frontend (React) to Backend (FastAPI): User registration data (email, full name, role, company name). (inferred from D023)
Backend (FastAPI) to PostgreSQL: User session data storage and retrieval. (D012, D013)
Backend (FastAPI) to PostgreSQL (via Serverless VPC Access): Application data storage and retrieval. (D009, D026)
Backend (FastAPI) to Frontend (React): Project dashboard data (active user metrics, system status, project lists). (inferred from D032)
interface_contracts: Firebase Authentication API: External API for user authentication, supporting Google sign-in and email/password login methods. Input: User credentials or social login tokens; Output: Authentication token, user profile data. (D016, D018)

Backend API `/api/v1/register` (inferred): REST endpoint accepting a POST request. Payload: JSON object with required fields `email` (string), `full_name` (string), `role` (string), `company_name` (string). (inferred from D023)

Backend API for Project Dashboard (e.g., `/api/v1/dashboard` - inferred): REST endpoint returning a JSON object containing `active_user_metrics` (object), `system_status` (object/string), and `project_lists` (array of objects). (inferred from D032)

constraints.text: The Version 1 Minimum Viable Product (MVP) must be delivered by September 1st, due to a planned marketing campaign launch on September 5th.
The PostgreSQL database instance SHALL NOT be exposed to the public internet for security reasons.
Firebase configuration keys MUST be provided by July 8th.
The system must achieve a 'super fast' performance level.

architectural_decisions.text: The frontend development stack will utilize React.
The backend service development stack will utilize Python with FastAPI.
PostgreSQL will be used as the standard relational database.
For Version 1, standard PostgreSQL database sessions will be used for managing user sessions.
Firebase Authentication will be integrated for user authentication in Version 1.
The React frontend application will be deployed on Vercel.
The FastAPI backend application will be deployed on Cloud Run, with the PostgreSQL database instance placed in a private VPC and connected via Serverless VPC Access.

open_analysis_questions: What are the specific data points, data types, and granular details for 'active user metrics', 'system status', and 'project lists' to be displayed on the project dashboard? (D031, D032, D033)
What are the concrete, measurable performance targets for the 'super fast' requirement (e.g., response times, concurrency limits)? (D005)

stakeholder.business_summary: The Phoenix Project is targeting a September 1st launch for its initial version (MVP) to align with a critical marketing campaign. This first version will include essential features like user login and a functional project dashboard, designed to be exceptionally fast for a smooth user experience. We are focused on delivering these core capabilities on time.
key_impact: Ensures the project launches on time for the planned September 5th marketing campaign.
Delivers a core set of features, including secure user login and a basic project dashboard, enabling initial user engagement.
Prioritizes fast system performance, enhancing user satisfaction and adoption.
Simplifies the user registration process, requiring only essential B2B (business-to-business) information: email, full name, role, and company name.
Establishes robust security measures to protect user data and ensure the database is not publicly accessible.
decisions_needing_alignment: Final detailed features and design for the project dashboard need to be confirmed with the marketing team next Wednesday.
major_unresolved_questions: What are the specific acceptance criteria for user registration beyond the confirmed data fields?
What are the detailed features and metrics that will be displayed on the project dashboard?

issues.location: QA View: Acceptance Criteria and Test Scenarios
issues.description: The acceptance criterion 'Given a new user navigates to the registration page, When they attempt to register with an email address that is already in use, Then the system prevents registration and displays a clear error message indicating the email is taken.' and its corresponding test scenario are not explicitly traceable to any extracted decision. While a common and good practice, it introduces a specific behavior not discussed in the meeting notes.
issues.suggestion: Remove or rephrase to indicate this is an assumed best practice, or explicitly state it's an inferred requirement that needs confirmation.

issues.description: The open question 'PM/QA: What are the password complexity requirements for email-based user registration (e.g., minimum length, requirement for numbers/symbols, uppercase/lowercase)?' was not raised in the meeting and is not traceable to an extracted decision. While a valid and important question, presenting it as an 'unresolved behavior' implies it was discussed and left open, which is not supported by the source.
issues.suggestion: Remove this question from 'unresolved_behaviors' or move it to a section explicitly for new questions identified during review, distinct from those that arose during the meeting.

issues.description: The `open_technical_questions` field is empty. However, the QA View identified several relevant 'unresolved_behaviors' (e.g., quantifiable performance targets for 'super fast', validation rules for 'Role' field) that have technical implications and would be valuable for engineers to be aware of, and the PM view also listed open questions.
issues.suggestion: Populate the `open_technical_questions` section with relevant unresolved items that have technical implications, drawing from the QA and PM views or directly from open questions in the extracted decisions (e.g., D019 on user registration acceptance criteria, D031 on detailed dashboard metrics).

issues.description: The Stakeholder View uses some technical jargon such as 'private VPC' and 'Serverless VPC Access' without providing a brief explanation, which might make it less accessible to a non-technical audience.
issues.suggestion: Consider adding a very brief, high-level explanation for such terms or rephrasing them to be more business-oriented for a broader stakeholder audience.

review.reviewer_notes: The report provides a comprehensive and generally accurate overview of the meeting decisions across all role views. Minor over-inferences were noted in the QA view regarding unstated requirements and an unraised question. The Engineer view could be more comprehensive by including relevant open technical questions. The Stakeholder view is mostly clear but could simplify some technical jargon. No critical issues were found, and no prompt injection or sensitive content was detected.
